name: Helium
name_origin: From Greek 'helios' (sun), reflecting its discovery as a spectral line in sunlight before it was found on Earth.

discovery.discoverer: Pierre Janssen and Norman Lockyer (spectroscopic); William Ramsay isolated it on Earth in 1895

occurrence.abundance_summary: Helium is the second most abundant element in the universe (~24% by mass) but rare on Earth (~5.2 ppm in atmosphere) because it escapes gravity. Commercial helium is extracted from natural gas wells where it accumulates from radioactive decay of heavy elements.
states: abundant in stars and gas giants

compounds.name: Helium hydride cation
compounds.summary: An exotic cation believed to be the first molecule to form in the early universe, detected in nebulae but unstable in normal conditions.

uses: Cryogenic coolant for superconducting magnets in MRI scanners and particle accelerators
Lifting gas for blimps, weather balloons, and party balloons
Inert shielding gas for arc welding of reactive metals
Pressurant for liquid-fueled rocket propellant tanks
Breathing gas mixtures (heliox/trimix) for deep-sea diving
Leak detection in vacuum systems and pressurized equipment
reactivity_summary: Helium is the least chemically reactive element and forms no stable neutral compounds under normal conditions. Its closed 1s2 shell gives it the highest first ionization energy of any element. A few exotic species (e.g., HeH+, Na2He at extreme pressures) have been reported but are not stable in everyday environments.
isotopes_summary: Helium has two stable isotopes: 4He (~99.9999%) and 3He (~1.4 ppm of natural helium). 3He is highly valued for cryogenics (sub-Kelvin dilution refrigerators) and as a potential fusion fuel; it is also used in neutron detectors.

citations.source: Royal Society of Chemistry - Periodic Table: Helium
